input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.reasoningText: The user wants me to benchmark three methods (AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, UniqueWordCount) from a TextAnalyzer project, comparing .NET 9 vs .NET 10 performance. Let me start by exploring the codebase and checking what .NET versions are available.

data.content: Good — both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0.
data.reasoningText:
Now I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that benchmarks the three methods (AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, UniqueWordCount) across both .NET 9 and .NET 10. I'll create a benchmark project that multi-targets both frameworks.

Let me create a benchmark project.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-95efe162a3394d78a3e8bd596a91fad2/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-95efe162a3394d78a3e8bd596a91fad2/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-95efe162a3394d78a3e8bd596a91fad2/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.0" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.reasoningText: I need to specify a framework for the host process. BenchmarkDotNet will handle running both runtimes. Let me use net10.0 as the host since it can orchestrate both.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't have a .NET 10 moniker yet. I'll configure jobs manually using `NuGetReferenceAttribute` approach or custom toolchain. Actually, let me use a simpler approach — configure the TFMs via `[SimpleJob]` with custom runtime configuration, or just use the `--runtimes` CLI argument.
data.reasoningText:
The BenchmarkDotNet version 0.14.0 doesn't have `RuntimeMoniker.Net100` yet. Let me check what monikers are available or use a newer version of BenchmarkDotNet. Alternatively, I can configure jobs manually.

Let me try using a custom job configuration with explicit runtime specification instead of the moniker.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't support .NET 10 yet. Let me write a lightweight manual benchmark instead.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.0 doesn't support .NET 10 at all with its SDK validator. Let me try a different approach: use a newer/nightly version of BenchmarkDotNet, or bypass the validation. Actually, let me try BenchmarkDotNet 0.15.0 or a nightly build that might support .NET 10.

Actually, let me try a simpler approach: write a manual benchmark instead of using BenchmarkDotNet, since BenchmarkDotNet doesn't support .NET 10 in the 0.14.0 release. I'll create a simple benchmarking harness that uses Stopwatch, compile it for both TFMs, and run each separately.


Given the time constraint, this manual approach makes sense and should be quick to implement.
